era_context: The early Iron Age II in the Levant and Eastern Mediterranean. The Neo-Assyrian Empire is in its formative phase under Ashur-dan II (r. 934–912 BCE), beginning territorial consolidation. In Greece, the Geometric period dominates; the alphabet has not yet been adopted. Egypt is in the late 21st Dynasty, fragmented between Tanite kings in the north and Theban high priests in the south. The Zhou Dynasty in China is in its middle period, with regional states consolidating power.

events.title: Ashur-dan II consolidates Assyrian power in northern Mesopotamia
events.description: Ashur-dan II (r. 934–912 BCE) continues his military campaigns to restore Assyrian territorial control after the period of weakness in the late 10th century. By 914 BCE, he has secured the heartland of Assyria and begun campaigns into the Euphrates valley and toward the Levantine coast. These campaigns lay the groundwork for the later Neo-Assyrian Empire's expansion. His reign marks the beginning of Assyria's resurgence as a major power.

sources.name: Assyrian King List and royal inscriptions (Ashur-dan II's annals)

events.certainty_note: Ashur-dan II's reign dates are well-established (934–912 BCE) from king lists and inscriptions. Specific campaigns in 914 BCE are inferred from the sequence of his annals, but exact year-by-year attribution is approximate.

disconfirming_evidence: No disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the significance of Ashur-dan II's reign has been somewhat overshadowed in older historiography by the later, more dramatic conquests of Ashurnasirpal II and Shalmaneser III; modern scholarship has revised upward the importance of this consolidation phase.
historiographic_note: The historical record for Assyria in this period is relatively strong, based on king lists, royal inscriptions, and later chronicles. However, Egyptian and Levantine sources for this specific year are minimal. Greek sources are non-existent for this period. The record is heavily skewed toward Mesopotamian political and military history; economic, social, and cultural developments are poorly documented. Archaeological evidence from this period is sparse and unevenly distributed.

graph_edges.note: Ashur-dan II's consolidation continued until his death in 912 BCE, when Ashurnasirpal II took the throne and dramatically accelerated Assyrian expansion.
